questions.question: Explain Lambert's Cosine Law in the context of diffuse reflection. How does it affect the brightness of a surface?
keyPoints: Lambert's cosine law states intensity is proportional to cos(θ) where θ is angle between surface normal and light direction
Mathematical form: Id = Kd × Il × cos(θ) = Kd × Il × (N·L)
Maximum intensity when surface directly faces light (θ=0°)
Zero intensity when surface is perpendicular to light (θ≥90°)
This is why surfaces appear darker when light strikes at grazing angles

questions.question: Differentiate between Gouraud Shading and Phong Shading. Which one produces better visual results and why?
keyPoints: Gouraud: computes illumination at vertices, interpolates colors across polygon
Phong: interpolates normals across polygon, computes illumination per pixel
Phong produces sharper, more accurate specular highlights
Gouraud may miss or distort specular highlights due to vertex-based computation
Phong is computationally more expensive but visually superior for shiny surfaces

questions.question: List and briefly explain the three components of the Phong Reflection Model.
keyPoints: Ambient (Ka × Ia): simulates indirect light, provides minimum brightness in shadow areas
Diffuse (Kd × Il × N·L): models matte surfaces, light scattered equally in all directions
Specular (Ks × Il × (R·V)^n): creates shiny highlights, depends on view direction
Each component uses reflection coefficients (Ka, Kd, Ks) between 0 and 1
Shininess exponent n controls highlight sharpness

questions.question: What is light attenuation? Why is it important in illumination models?
keyPoints: Light attenuation describes how light intensity decreases with distance from source
Typical formula: Attenuation = 1 / (a0 + a1×d + a2×d²)
Simulates real-world physics where light spreads over larger area
Without attenuation, distant objects would appear as bright as nearby ones
Prevents unrealistically bright scenes and adds depth perception

questions.question: Define BRDF (Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function) and explain its significance in realistic rendering.
keyPoints: BRDF is a function: fr(ωi, ωo) that describes surface reflectance
Takes incoming light direction (ωi) and outgoing view direction (ωo)
Returns ratio of reflected radiance to incoming irradiance
Encodes material properties like roughness, metalness, anisotropy
Foundation of physically-based rendering (PBR)

questions.question: Explain the three main types of light sources used in computer graphics.
keyPoints: Point Light: emits from a single point in all directions, intensity decreases with distance
Directional Light: parallel rays from infinite distance, no attenuation (like sunlight)
Spotlight: emits light in a cone with specific angle, includes falloff within cone
Each has position/direction and color/intensity properties
Choice affects realism and computational cost

questions.question: Derive and explain the complete Phong Reflection Model equation. Discuss how each parameter affects the final rendered appearance of a surface.
keyPoints: Complete equation: I = Ka×Ia + Kd×Il×max(0,N·L) + Ks×Il×max(0,R·V)^n
Ambient term Ka controls base illumination in shadows (0-1 range)
Diffuse term: Kd controls how much light is diffusely scattered, N·L is Lambert cosine term
Specular term: Ks controls highlight intensity, n (shininess) controls highlight sharpness
Higher n = smaller sharper highlight (polished), lower n = larger diffuse highlight (matte)
Material appearance controlled by selecting appropriate coefficients
Viewing angle affects specular term through R·V dot product

questions.question: Compare Flat Shading, Gouraud Shading, and Phong Shading in terms of computation method, visual quality, and computational cost. Under what circumstances would you choose each method?
keyPoints: Flat Shading: one calculation per polygon, faceted look, fastest
Gouraud Shading: vertex calculations + color interpolation, smooth but may miss highlights
Phong Shading: normal interpolation + per-pixel calculation, best quality but expensive
Quality order: Flat < Gouraud < Phong
Cost order: Flat < Gouraud < Phong
Choose Flat for real-time with many polygons, Gouraud for animated surfaces, Phong for photo-realism
Hardware support makes Phong common in modern graphics pipelines

questions.question: Explain the physics behind specular reflection. How does the shininess exponent (n) affect the appearance of the specular highlight? Provide examples of surfaces with different shininess values.
keyPoints: Specular reflection occurs on shiny surfaces where light reflects primarily in one direction
Reflection vector R is computed using: R = 2(N·L)N - L
Specular intensity = Ks × Il × (R·V)^n
Higher n (100-200): concentrated highlight (polished metal, plastic)
Medium n (20-50): moderate highlight (wood, cloth)
Lower n (1-10): very diffuse highlight (rough surfaces)
R·V term means specular highlight only visible when view aligns with reflection
This is why you see moving highlight when rotating object

questions.question: What is the Fresnel effect? How is it implemented in illumination models, and why is it important for realistic rendering?
keyPoints: Fresnel effect: surfaces become more reflective at grazing (oblique) viewing angles
Physical basis: more light reflects off surface at shallow angles, less transmits through
Implementation: F(θ) = F₀ + (1-F₀)(1-cosθ)^5 or similar approximations
Where F₀ is reflectance at normal incidence (typically 0.02-0.05 for dielectrics)
Critical for realistic rendering of water, glass, metal surfaces
Explains why pool edges look more reflective at edges, why car paint shimmers
Combined with other lighting models for photorealistic results
Modern PBR systems incorporate Fresnel for material realism
